title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
Paul O. Hedman
Thomas H. Fletcher
Daniel V. Flores
Stewart G. Graham
Jason K. Haslam
Robert L. Murray
G. Wayne Timothy
Department of Chemical Engineering and
Advanced Combustion Engineering Research
Center,
Brigham Young University,
Provo, UT 84602
Observations of Flame Behavior
in a Laboratory-Scale Premixed
Natural Gas/Air Gas Turbine
Combustor From Planar Laser
Induced Fluorescence
Measurements of OH, Laser
Doppler Anemometer Velocity
Measurements, and Coherent
Anti-Stokes Roman Spectrometer
Temperature Measurements
Instantaneous planar laser induced ﬂuorescence (PLIF) images of OH, simultaneous
axial/radial and axial/tangential velocity measurements with an LDA (laser Doppler
anemometer), and instantaneous gas temperature measurements using a coherent anti-
Stokes Raman spectrometer (CARS) were obtained in a pre-mixed, swirl-stabilized
laboratory-scale combustor, ﬁred with natural gas. Four operating conditions were ex-
amined (two swirl conditions and equivalence ratios of 0.80 and 0.65). The instantaneous
PLIF images of OH illustrate the chaotic nature of the turbulent ﬂame eddies. Mean and
standard deviations of each variable were measured at different locations in the ﬂame,
along with probability density functions. Stability of the ﬂame at each condition is related
to measurements in the central and side recirculation zones. DOI: 10.1115/1.1914804

along with probability density functions. Stability of the ﬂame at each condition is related
to measurements in the central and side recirculation zones. DOI: 10.1115/1.1914804

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
Introduction
Lean, premixed combustion is being used as a means to meet
severe NOx and CO emission regulations on stationary gas tur-
bines. A series of laser-diagnostic experiments was performed in a
lean-premixed combustion apparatus at the same lean operating
conditions. Planar laser-induced ﬂuorescence PLIF was used to
image OH, two-component laser Doppler anemometry LDA 1
was used to measure velocity, and coherent anti-Stokes Raman
spectroscopy CARS 2 was used to measure temperature. These
data were obtained principally for evaluation of comprehensive
gas turbine combustion models 3–5. The laser diagnostic data
presented in this paper were obtained in a laboratory-scale, gas-
turbine combustor LSGTC with a pre-mixed, swirl-stabilized,
natural gas ﬂame. The LSGTC was operated at two swirl condi-
tions high swirl HS and medium swirl MS and two fuel
equivalence ratios 0.80 and 0.65 at atmospheric pressure. The
LSGTC simulates many of the key combustor characteristics of
commercial gas turbines 6, and provides a realistic ﬂame where
model predictions and in situ measurements can be compared. Use
of advanced optical diagnostics permits near-instantaneous about
10-ns resolution, nonintrusive measurements that provide insight
into the physical processes that govern the combustion processes.
This set of data is thought to be among the most complete for lean
premixed combustion applicable to gas turbines, and therefore
serves as a good test set for modern computer models.
Combustor Test Facility
The combustion facility used for this study is the same used by
Hedman and co-workers 7–11. The facility was further modiﬁed
for this study to allow investigation of a premixed natural gas/air
ﬂame. This premixed natural gas/air combustor was developed
with support from the US/DOE Advanced Turbine Systems pro-
gram, and is referred to as the ATS burner. The laboratory-scale,
gas-turbine combustor LSGTC, illustrated in Fig. 1, was de-
signed to reproduce the characteristics found in a modern annular
combustor 6. The combustor, which was provided to BYU by
Wright Laboratory 12, consists of a four-sided chamber with
metal corner ﬁllets to more nearly simulate an axisymmetric com-
bustor while maintaining adequate optical access. A metal plate or
a quartz window can be placed in each wall, depending on the
optical access required. The exit of the reactor was restricted by a
ﬂat plate with a 92-mm hole in the center.
The premixed swirl injector, illustrated in Fig. 2, is located in
the center of the combustion chamber base. The injector consists
of a stainless steel tube with a honeycombed brass insert. The
brass insert serves to smooth the ﬂow as well as provide a ﬂame
arrestor in case of ﬂashback in the premixed fuel/air mixture.
Inserts are installed in the injector to provide different magnitudes
of swirl to the inlet ﬂow. The swirler inserts are placed 40 mm
Contributed by the Fuels and Combustion Division of THE AMERICAN SOCIETY OF
MECHANICAL ENGINEERS for publication in the ASME JOURNAL OF ENGINEERING FOR
GAS TURBINES AND POWER. Manuscript received by the F&C Division July 30, 2003;
ﬁnal revision received May 5, 2004. Associate Editor: P. C. Malte.
724 / Vol. 127, OCTOBER 2005
Copyright © 2005 by ASME
Transactions of the ASME
Downloaded 15 Mar 2010 to 129.137.90.23. Redistribution subject to ASME license or copyright; see http://www.asme.org/terms/Terms_Use.cfm

above the brass honeycomb and secured by a threaded post that
anchors the swirler to the brass honeycomb. A cap with an 18-mm
hole in the center and beveled walls is placed on the top of the
steel tube to coalesce the swirling ﬂow at the injector exit. This
cap was needed in order to make the burner operate successfully.
Figure 2 shows a schematic of the two swirl inserts used in this
study. The inserts are labeled high swirl HS and medium swirl
MS to coincide with the 60- and 45-deg slot angles. The swirl
number SN indicated in Fig. 2 is a nondimensional number de-
ﬁned by the ratio of the axial ﬂux of tangential momentum di-
vided by the product of the axial ﬂux of axial momentum and the
equivalent nozzle radius 13.
Fuel ﬂow to the burner was measured with a variable area ﬂow
meter and is reported in slpm standard liters per minute, or
10−3 standard m3/min referenced to 1 atm 101 kPa and 70 °F
21.1 °C. Airﬂow was controlled by varying the upstream pres-
sure of a choked ﬂow nozzle. The diameter of the nozzle used for
this study was 0.1485 in. 3.77 mm providing airﬂow rates from
250 to 1000 slpm, depending on upstream pressure. Uniform mix-
ing of gaseous fuel and air was assured by a the length of the
tube from the mixing point to the burner, b a jet mixer in the
feed system, and c a honeycomb ﬂame arrestor. The air ﬂow rate
for the four test conditions used in this study was 500 slpm.
Visual Flame Characterization. Figure 3 presents a schematic
drawing of the ﬂow patterns present in the swirl-stabilized ﬂame.
The swirling ﬂow creates a funnel or tornado-like vortex ﬂow
centered on the injector exit. A central recirculation zone and a
side recirculation zone accompany the tornadolike ﬂow. Both re-
circulation zones have been found to inﬂuence ﬂame stability.
Depending on swirl number and fuel equivalence ratio, one or the
other of the recirculation zones may dominate the ﬂame stability
1.
Wide variations in ﬂame shape have been observed in the ATS
burner for different combinations of swirl number and fuel
equivalence ratio. These structures were studied both visually,
through photographic and video recordings, with the PLIF images,
and with the velocity and temperature results obtained in this
study. Such observations have yielded insight into the location of
the ﬂame front, variation of ﬂame structure with operating condi-
tions, and the presence and strength of the recirculation zones.
These ﬂame shapes are primarily a result of the effect of the swirl
intensity and fuel equivalence ratio on the ﬂow ﬁeld, with the
magnitude of the airﬂow rate being of secondary importance. The
equivalence ratio was varied by changing the fuel ﬂow rate while
maintaining a constant 500 slpm air ﬂow rate. The changes in
ﬂame shape were characterized using the point of attachment, the
location of the ﬂame front, the structure of the vortex, and the
visible intensity of the ﬂame.
An example of a visual image of the ﬂame structure is pre-
sented in Fig. 4 for a stoichiometric =1.0, MS case. The 
=1.0 image was included in the paper to provide a photographic
illustration of the ﬂame structure. The =1.0 image was used
because this image was brighter, more visual, and reproduced bet-
ter than images from ﬂames with 1.0. It was visually observed
that the ﬂame at =0.80 was more stable than at =0.65. The
ﬂame with the HS injector was more coalesced and closer to the
injector than with the MS injector. At =0.65, the ﬂame was near
the lean ﬂammability limit, and was quite unstable for both swirl
injectors. When using the MS injector, the ﬂame would oscillate
between two different ﬂame structures, one that was more or less
Fig. 1

Fig. 2

swirl block inserts „adapted from Schmidt and Hedman †11‡…

stabilized combustor „adapted from Gupta et al. †13‡…

attached to the vortex funnel, and one that was lifted well above
the vortex funnel. The MS case at =0.65 was at the very edge of
the lean ﬂammability limit, and on occasion would actually extin-
guish.
Planar Laser-Induced Fluorescence. Planar laser-induced
ﬂuorescence PLIF is a valuable tool for studying combustion
ph

text:
Introduction
Lean, premixed combustion is being used as a means to meet
severe NOx and CO emission regulations on stationary gas tur-
bines. A series of laser-diagnostic experiments was performed in a
lean-premixed combustion apparatus at the same lean operating
conditions. Planar laser-induced ﬂuorescence PLIF was used to
image OH, two-component laser Doppler anemometry LDA 1
was used to measure velocity, and coherent anti-Stokes Raman
spectroscopy CARS 2 was used to measure temperature. These
data were obtained principally for evaluation of comprehensive
gas turbine combustion models 3–5. The laser diagnostic data
presented in this paper were obtained in a laboratory-scale, gas-
turbine combustor LSGTC with a pre-mixed, swirl-stabilized,
natural gas ﬂame. The LSGTC was operated at two swirl condi-
tions high swirl HS and medium swirl MS and two fuel
equivalence ratios 0.80 and 0.65 at atmospheric pressure. The
LSGTC simulates many of the key combustor characteristics of
commercial gas turbines 6, and provides a realistic ﬂame where
model predictions and in situ measurements can be compared. Use
of advanced optical diagnostics permits near-instantaneous about
10-ns resolution, nonintrusive measurements that provide insight
into the physical processes that govern the combustion processes.
This set of data is thought to be among the most complete for lean
premixed combustion applicable to gas turbines, and therefore
serves as a good test set for modern computer models.
Combustor Test Facility
The combustion facility used for this study is the same used by
Hedman and co-workers 7–11. The facility was further modiﬁed
for this study to allow investigation of a premixed natural gas/air
ﬂame. This premixed natural gas/air combustor was developed
with support from the US/DOE Advanced Turbine Systems pro-
gram, and is referred to as the ATS burner. The laboratory-scale,
gas-turbine combustor LSGTC, illustrated in Fig. 1, was de-
signed to repro

hole in the center and beveled walls is placed on the top of the
steel tube to coalesce the swirling ﬂow at the injector exit. This
cap was needed in order to make the burner operate successfully.
Figure 2 shows a schematic of the two swirl inserts used in this
study. The inserts are labeled high swirl HS and medium swirl
MS to coincide with the 60- and 45-deg slot angles. The swirl
number SN indicated in Fig. 2 is a nondimensional number de-
ﬁned by the ratio of the axial ﬂux of tangential momentum di-
vided by the product of the axial ﬂux of axial momentum and the
equivalent nozzle radius 13.
Fuel ﬂow to the burner was measured with a variable area ﬂow
meter and is reported in slpm standard liters per minute, or

sure of a choked ﬂow nozzle. The diameter of the nozzle used for

250 to 1000 slpm, depending on upstream pressure. Uniform mix-
ing of gaseous fuel and air was assured by a the length of the
tube from the mixing point to the burner, b a jet mixer in the
feed system, and c a honeycomb ﬂame arrestor. The air ﬂow rate
for the four test conditions used in this study was 500 slpm.

drawing of the ﬂow patterns present in the swirl-stabilized ﬂame.
The swirling ﬂow creates a funnel or tornado-like vortex ﬂow
centered on the injector exit. A central recirculation zone and a
side recirculation zone accompany the tornadolike ﬂow. Both re-
circulation zones have been found to inﬂuence ﬂame stability.
Depending on swirl number and fuel equivalence ratio, one or the
other of the recirculation zones may dominate the ﬂame stability
1.

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
attached to the vortex funnel, and one that was lifted well above
the vortex funnel. The MS case at =0.65 was at the very edge of
the lean ﬂammability limit, and on occasion would actually extin-
guish.
Planar Laser-Induced Fluorescence. Planar laser-induced
ﬂuorescence PLIF is a valuable tool for studying combustion
phenomena. Flame propagation is partly governed by the diffusion
and transport of important combustion intermediates like OH and
CH. Laser-induced ﬂuorescence has the potential of measuring
trace amounts of these short-lived combustion species at near in-
stantaneous temporal resolution 10–100 ns. Likewise, because
PLIF is laser-based, probing of the burner is nonintrusive. Laser-
induced ﬂuorescence measurements are often made at the focal
point of a converging excitation laser 14. However, due to the
advances in detector and laser performance, ﬂuorescence can be
captured instantaneously over a two-dimensional area for many
species. The two-dimensional image area captured resembles an
instantaneous photograph-like image directly related to the con-
centration distribution of the combustion species being probed.
The ﬂame is probed with a thin sheet of laser light tuned to the
appropriate wavelength. The PLIF images of OH provide detailed
information regarding the location and shape of the ﬂame struc-
ture. This structural information is very useful in analyzing burner
designs, understanding combustion phenomena, and validating
combustion computer models.
The use of PLIF to determine ﬂame properties has become
more popular in the last few years. For instance, use of PLIF and
acetone tracers is a popular method to determine mixing charac-
teristics 15–18. Measurements of NO using PLIF are reviewed
by Cooper and Laurendeau 19. Measurements of OH using PLIF
to determine ﬂame structure in natural gas/air ﬂames 20–25 and
kerosene spray ﬂames 26,27 related to gas turbine combustors
have also been reported recently reported. However, none of thes

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
256 separate laser shots for the OH species for the four test con-
ditions. The mean image is very similar to the observed video
images. However, the variation in pixel count, as reﬂected in the
normalized gray image, allows more insight into the structure of
the ﬂame and the character of the two different recirculation
zones. As with the video images and instantaneous PLIF images,
the mean PLIF images from the =0.80 cases indicate a very
structured stable ﬂame for both the HS and MS injectors. The
vortex for the HS case is narrower, and the top of the ﬂame zone
is closer to the injector than in the MS case. The =0.65 cases
reﬂect a narrower vortex and lower ﬂame zone for the HS con-
ﬁguration. Both conﬁgurations are near lean blowout at =0.65,
but the MS case is more unstable, and consequently more likely to
extinguish than the HS case. Standard deviation PLIF images
were also calculated pixel by pixel and are shown in Fig. 7 for the
four cases. The standard deviation gives insight into the amount of
large-scale turbulence and zones where ignition/extinction occur
within the ﬂame. The greater coalescence in the ﬂows with the HS
injector is more readily apparent in the standard deviation images
see Fig. 7, images B and D compared to images A and C, respec-
tively. The greater instability associated with the MS =0.65
case is also apparent in Fig. 7, image C. As noted above, the
normalized transform from the detector counts has a different
maximum value for each image. These normalized images there-
fore provide means for comparison of the shape of the ﬂow ﬁeld,
as depicted by the standard deviation calculation, rather than the
relative intensity.
The actual detector counts at selected pixel locations have been
used to create PDFs within the ﬂame structure. The average count
and peak count, and the shape of the PDFs provide additional
insights into the ﬂame characteristics, and have provided a mea-
sure of the effect of operating condition on the relative level

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
MS, =0.65 are presented in Figs. 8 and 9, respectively. Eight
locations where the PDFs have been determined are superimposed
on the mean PLIF image for both cases presented. The eight lo-
cations shown in Figs. 8 and 9 are identical for both cases. Loca-
tions were selected above the ﬂame, in the most intense regions of
the ﬂame, and below the ﬂame. The PDF plots appear to be more
similar to beta distributions rather than Gaussian distributions. In
every case, the PDF shows that there is a signiﬁcant amount of
time that no OH exists at that location e.g., Fig. 8, even in the
most intense part of the ﬂame zone. Likewise, signiﬁcant levels of
OH are seen even in regions that are predominately outside, but
near the ﬂame zone. In Fig. 8 HS, =0.80 the maximum detec-
tor count 4633 counts at maximum, 1652 counts at average oc-
curs at a location inside the intense part of the ﬂame location D.
In Fig. 9 MS, =0.65, the maximum detector count in the in-
tense part of the ﬂame 1926 counts at maximum, 402 counts at
average occurs at location B. This suggests that the concentration
of OH in the MS, =0.65 case is about one fourth that of the HS,
=0.80 case. The nonlinear PLIF response and the possibility of
ﬂuorescence saturation may alter the interpretation of concentra-
tion slightly. Nevertheless, the magnitude of the detector counts,
as well as the corresponding PDFs, provides considerable insight
into the character of the turbulent, reacting ﬂame.
Figure 9 further quantiﬁes the unstable nature of the MS, 
=0.65 case. Even in the most intense region location B, there is
a considerable amount of time when no OH is detected in the
central vortex region. It is apparent that the stabilization process
in the turbulent, swirl-stabilized ﬂame is a very complicated pro-
cess. The PLIF images obtained have provided some insight into
this process, but must be considered with the velocity and tem-
perature data to provide a more complete understanding about the
tu

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
transmitting lens was 762 mm with the beams intersecting at a
half angle of 3.1 deg. The diameter of the laser beam at the lens
face was approximately 4 mm. This two-color, four-beam LDA
conﬁguration formed a probe volume with two fringe patterns at
right angles to each other, with a blue fringe spacing of 4.44 m
and a green fringe spacing of 4.68 m.
The airﬂow was seeded with about 6-m diameter hollow alu-
minum oxide micro-spheres. The aerodynamic diameter of these
particles was such that they followed the gas ﬂow patterns, even
in the recirculation zones. The seeding of the airﬂow occurred
prior to the fuel addition point and well before the fuel injector. A
particle traversing the probe volume produced two Doppler sig-
nals. The fringes of the blue beams gave Doppler signals for the
axial velocity component and the fringes of the green beams gave
the Doppler signals that corresponded to either the radial or tan-
gential velocity component. Sample rates ranged from 400 counts
per second to 10,000 counts per second, depending on where the
probe volume was positioned in the ﬂame. Sample rates were
signiﬁcantly lower in the high-temperature regions of the ﬂame.
The Doppler signals were collected in two different photo-
multiplier tubes PMT. Each PMT was connected to a signal
processor 30 for ﬁltering and validation. Valid velocity signals
were recorded and stored by computer using a direct memory
access DMA digital I/O card from TSI and a data acquisition
program 31 also developed by TSI.
Figures 10 and 11 provide example axial, radial, and tangential
iso-velocity contour plots for the HS =0.80 and the MS 
=0.65 cases, respectively. The results for the other two cases are
reported by Murray 1. The analysis of the approximately 4000
instantaneous velocities obtained at each measurement location in
the ﬂame zone allows statistical investigation into the turbulent
character of the ﬂame to be made. Example standard deviations
in m/s for the axial, radia

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
data. Figures 14 and 15 show examples of axial velocity PDFs at
an axial position of 80 mm and radial positions from near the
centerline to about a 40-mm radial location for the HS =0.80
and MS =0.65 cases, respectively. Additional PDF results are
provided by Murray 1. Labeled on the PDF are the axial posi-
tion, radial position, mean axial velocity, and standard deviation.
Generally, the axial velocity PDFs have a near Gaussian distribu-
tion in an identiﬁable ﬂow zone. However, these PDFs had a
bimodal distribution when gas velocities were measured across
the shear layer between two different identiﬁable ﬂow zones.
Typically, this can be seen at a position near the zero velocity
contour. These measurements have velocity components from
both sides of the zero velocity boundary.
In Fig. 14, starting at a radial position of −1 mm, the PDF has
a basic Gaussian proﬁle with a slightly increased gradient in the
positive velocity region. As measurements move to a radial posi-
tion of 11 mm, the PDF became less Gaussian, with the positive
velocity region of the PDF increasing in size, the negative veloc-
ity region decreasing in size, and the standard deviation increas-
ing. At 17 mm, the positive velocity region of the PDF has in-
creased signiﬁcantly. At radial positions of 23 and 29 mm, the
positive velocity region of the PDF continues to increase, the
mean axial velocity becomes positive overall, the standard devia-
tion continues to increase, and the bimodal peaks are about equal
in size and shape. It should be noted that these two positions 23
and 29 mm straddle the zero axial velocity contour, and hence
have velocity components from both sides of the zero velocity
boundary. At a radial position of 35 mm, the bimodal distribution
continues, the positive region of the PDF increases, the positive
mean axial velocity increases in magnitude, and the standard de-
viation remains fairly constant. At a radial position of 41 mm, the
negative velocity region of the PDF

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
conclusion of strong shear layers in the combustion chamber. Bi-
modal distributions across the shear layer zero axial velocity con-
tour were typically found from an axial position of 60 mm to an
axial position of 100 mm. Below an axial position of 60 mm
bimodal distributions were sometimes seen, but the spacing be-
tween measurements was too great to get a good progression of
PDFs. At axial positions above 125 mm, the measured velocities
across the shear layer were not high enough to demonstrate the
bimodal distributions in the PDFs. Instead, the PDF usually rep-
resented a near Gaussian distribution with the peak of the distri-
bution being at or near the axial centerline.

text:
conclusion of strong shear layers in the combustion chamber. Bi-
modal distributions across the shear layer zero axial velocity con-
tour were typically found from an axial position of 60 mm to an
axial position of 100 mm. Below an axial position of 60 mm
bimodal distributions were sometimes seen, but the spacing be-
tween measurements was too great to get a good progression of
PDFs. At axial positions above 125 mm, the measured velocities
across the shear layer were not high enough to demonstrate the
bimodal distributions in the PDFs. Instead, the PDF usually rep-
resented a near Gaussian distribution with the peak of the distri-
bution being at or near the axial centerline.

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
Discussion of Recirculation Zones. Recirculation zones play a
major role in stabilizing a turbulent, premixed natural gas com-
bustor. In this study, it was assumed that gas moving down into
the ignition zone and radially towards the axial centerline im-
proved the stability of the ﬂame. In other words, the incoming
premixed fuel and air needed a source of heat and/or radicals from
the recirculated products to stabilize the ﬂame. The intensity of
the recirculation zones was based on how well the recirculation
zones moved hot gases to the middle of the combustion chamber
and down into the ignition zone. Examination of the zero axial
velocity and zero radial velocity contours helps deﬁne recircula-
tion regions. Zero velocity contours for both the axial and radial
velocity maps are shown in both Fig. 10 and 11. The sizes of the
recirculation zones are directly related to the heights and widths of
the zero velocity contours. For convenience, a set of dimensions is
deﬁned in Fig. 10 and 11. The maximum height of the zero axial
velocity contour, measured from the combustor base plate is la-
beled “hcu” for the central recirculation zone CRZ and “hsu” for
Fig. 8
Example PDFs from OH-PLIF images „HS injector, =0.80…

Transactions of the ASME
Downloaded 15 Mar 2010 to 129.137.90.23. Redistribution subject to ASME license or copyright; see http://www.asme.org/terms/Terms_Use.cfm

the side recirculation zone SRZ. The maximum horizontal dis-
tance between the zero axial velocity contours for the CRZ is the
width of the axial ﬂow channel wcu, with a similar value wsu
for the SRZ. Murray and co-workers 1,29 have used the heights
and widths to quantitatively investigate the effect of ﬂow recircu-
lation on ﬂame stability. The measured axial velocities were radi-
ally averaged inside both the central and side recirculation zones
for each case. The resultant volumetric ﬂow rates are shown at
each axial position in Fig. 16 and 17.
The conclusions drawn are 1 the MS cases had a higher ﬂow
rate in the CRZ at an axial position of 120–150 mm than observed
in the corresponding HS cases, 2 the HS cases had a higher Qc at

an axial position of 50–120 mm than observed in the correspond-
ing MS cases, and 3 the MS =0.80 case had about the same
CRZ ﬂow rate as the MS =0.65 case up to an axial position of
105 mm above the base of the combustion chamber.
The ﬂow rates through the SRZ were much greater and ex-
tended higher up into the combustion chamber for the MS cases
than the HS cases. The ﬂow rates were only calculated out to a
radial position of about 40–45 mm. Some of the cases had a SRZ
that extended beyond a radial position of 40–45 mm. For these
cases, the ﬂow rate could only be determined up to the farthest
radial position where data were taken, hence the values shown in
Fig. 17 should be larger in most cases. Figure 17 shows that the
MS cases had greater SRZ ﬂow rates than the HS cases, with the
largest ﬂow rate in the MS =0.80 case. The MS =0.80 case
was the only case that had the SRZ extend up into the visible
ﬂame zone.
The HS cases had larger CRZ ﬂow rates than the MS cases at
axial positions of 10–110 mm. The ignition zone was located be-
low an axial position of 50 mm and the lower half of the visible
ﬂame zone was located from 50 to 110 mm. Therefore, HS cases
had a larger CRZ ﬂow rate, recirculating hot gases down through
the lower half of the visible ﬂame zone and into the ignition zone

than for the MS cases. In addition, the MS case did not have a
change in the CRZ until after an axial position of 105 mm. This
suggested that the CRZ played only a small role in stabilizing the
MS cases. Large increases in the CRZ ﬂow rate appear to occur in
the visible ﬂame zone. These large increases in the CRZ ﬂow rate
could partly be due to an increase in temperature. Further studies
need to be made to identify the relationship between recirculated
ﬂow rates and temperature.
The MS cases had much larger SRZ ﬂow rates than the HS
cases in spite of limitations in completely calculating the SRZ
ﬂow rates for the MS cases. The MS =0.80 case was the only
case where the SRZ extended up into the visible ﬂame zone. It
appears that the MS cases needed to have more recirculated hot
gases coming down into the ignition zone to stabilize the ﬂame.
These conclusions support observations made earlier. However,
only the MS =0.80 case extended up into the visible ﬂame zone
and thus only the MS =0.80 case had improved stability from
the stronger side recirculation.
Gas Temperature Measurements
Coherent anti-Stokes spectroscopy CARS is based on Raman
scattering of high-energy, coherent, laser beams. In general, a
pump beam is split into two beams of equal intensity, and crossed
with a Stokes beam that is tuned to the particular Raman reso-
nance for the species being probed e.g., 2331 cm−1 for N2 and
2143 cm−1 for CO. Nonlinear wave mixing occurs in the diag-
nostic volume where the three probe beams cross, and a fourth
beam is generated at the anti-Stokes frequency for the species
being probed. The anti-Stokes spectra contain information that
relates to gas temperature and species concentration. The nonlin-
ear wave mixing is a vector process, and the laser-like anti-Stokes
signal leaves the diagnostic volume in a prescribed direction that
depends on the vector angles of the pump and Stokes beams.
Since the signal is laser-like, it can be focused into a spectrometer
for analysis. A detailed discussion of CARS theory and applica-
tion can be found in Eckbreth 14.
Several types of CARS setups have been explored previously
by Hedman and co-workers 32–35 in combustion systems. The
current BYU CARS instrument 2,36 uses a frequency doubled
ND:Yag laser 532 nm to supply the two pump beams. A portion
of the 532-nm laser energy is split off and used to excite the
Stokes laser. A broadband, dual pyromethene dye laser py-
romethene 597 and 650 from Exciton is used to simultaneously
excite the Raman frequencies of N2, CO, O2, and CO2. The spec-
trometer used in this study was patterned after the one used by
Eckbreth at UTRC 14,37. The diagnostic volume had a 200
-m waist and a 1-mm length. The spectra were imaged onto an
intensiﬁed photodiode array IPDA of an EG&G PARC 1421B
camera, and recorded to a computer via an EG&G OMA model
1461. The speciﬁc characteristics of the IPDA and OMA system
were determined by Boyack and co-worker 32,33. Gas tempera-
tures were determined from the N2 CARS spectra using the FT-
CARS code 38 from SANDIA National Laboratories with modi-
ﬁed input and output interfaces. Spectra that were too noisy or that
could not be ﬁt well with the FTCARS code were discarded. Flores
and co-workers 2,36 conducted temperature calibration mea-
surements on air in an electrically heated tube furnace equipped
with a type-K thermocouple and a digital readout. Measurements
in the range of 300–1000 K resulted in an average error of 30 K
and standard deviations of 50 K. Accuracy of the temperature
measurements at higher temperatures is thought to be within the
usual ±50 K generally assumed for the CARS technique. Addi-
tional details of the instrumentation and c

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
Discussion of Recirculation Zones. Recirculation zones play a
major role in stabilizing a turbulent, premixed natural gas com-
bustor. In this study, it was assumed that gas moving down into
the ignition zone and radially towards the axial centerline im-
proved the stability of the ﬂame. In other words, the incoming
premixed fuel and air needed a source of heat and/or radicals from
the recirculated products to stabilize the ﬂame. The intensity of
the recirculation zones was based on how well the recirculation
zones moved hot gases to the middle of the combustion chamber
and down into the ignition zone. Examination of the zero axial
velocity and zero radial velocity contours helps deﬁne recircula-
tion regions. Zero velocity contours for both the axial and radial
velocity maps are shown in both Fig. 10 and 11. The sizes of the
recirculation zones are directly related to the heights and widths of
the zero velocity contours. For convenience, a set of dimensions is
deﬁned in Fig. 10 and 11. The maximum height of the zero axial
velocity contour, measured from the combustor base plate is la-
beled “hcu” for the central recirculation zone CRZ and “hsu” for
Fig. 8
Example PDFs from OH-PLIF images „HS injector, =0.80…

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
the side recirculation zone SRZ. The maximum horizontal dis-
tance between the zero axial velocity contours for the CRZ is the
width of the axial ﬂow channel wcu, with a similar value wsu
for the SRZ. Murray and co-workers 1,29 have used the heights
and widths to quantitatively investigate the effect of ﬂow recircu-
lation on ﬂame stability. The measured axial velocities were radi-
ally averaged inside both the central and side recirculation zones
for each case. The resultant volumetric ﬂow rates are shown at
each axial position in Fig. 16 and 17.
The conclusions drawn are 1 the MS cases had a higher ﬂow
rate in the CRZ at an axial position of 120–150 mm than observed
in the corresponding HS cases, 2 the HS cases had a higher Qc at

ing MS cases, and 3 the MS =0.80 case had about the same

105 mm above the base of the combustion chamber.
The ﬂow rates through the SRZ were much greater and ex-

ﬂame zone was located from 50 to 110 mm. Therefore, HS cases
had a larger CRZ ﬂow rate, recirculating hot gases down through

change in the CRZ until after an axial position of 105 mm. This
suggested that the CRZ played only a small role in stabilizing the

Gas Temperature Measurements
Coherent anti-Stokes spectroscopy CARS is based on Raman
scattering of high-energy, coherent, laser beams. In general, a
pump beam is split into two beams of equal intensity, and crossed
with a Stokes beam that is tuned to the particular Raman reso-
nance for the species being probed e.g., 2331 cm−1 for N2 and
2143 cm−1 for CO. Nonlinear wave mixing occurs in the diag-
nostic volume where the three probe beams cross, and a fourth
beam is generated at the anti-Stokes frequency for the species
being probed. The anti-Stokes spectra contain information that
relates to gas temperature and species concentration. The nonlin-
ear wave mixing is a vector process, and the laser-like anti-Stokes
signal leaves the diagnostic volume in a prescribed direction that
depends on the vector angles of the pump and Stokes beams.
Since the signal is laser-like, it can be focused into a spectrometer
for an

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
show that the ﬂame in the MS =0.65 case is higher in the com-
bustion chamber when compared to the HS =0.80 most stable
ﬂame case. The peak temperature of the MS =0.65 least stable
ﬂame case was also lower 1449 versus 1648 K than the HS 
=0.80 case. The adiabatic ﬂame temperatures were 2000 and 1750
K for the =0.80 and =0.65 cases, respectively. The differences
between measured and adiabatic ﬂame temperature are attributed
to the heat losses from the burner.
Comparison of Figs. 18a and 18b, or Figs. 18c and 18d
show that the increased heat release associated with the higher
fuel equivalence ratio 0.80 versus 0.65 had the effect of increas-
ing ﬂame stability, causing the vortex core to be closer to the
injector inlet. The increased heat release also causes a signiﬁcant
increase in the peak ﬂame temperature.
The effect of swirl on ﬂame structure can be seen by comparing
Figs. 18a and 18c or Figs. 18b and 18d. The higher swirl
level has increased ﬂame stability as evidenced by 1 the location
of the ﬂame vortex HS moves the vortex closer to the injector,
and 2 the higher peak ﬂame temperatures associated with the
higher swirl level 1550 versus 1449 K for the =0.65 cases, and
1648 versus 1603 K for the =0.80 cases.
As noted above, recirculation zones play a major role in stabi-
lizing a turbulent, premixed natural gas combustor. Gas moving
down into the ignition zone and radially towards the axial center-
line improved the stability of the ﬂame. The intensity of the recir-
culation zones was based on how well the recirculation zones
moved hot gases to the middle of the combustion chamber and
down into the ignition zone. The vortex structures shown in the
gas temperature maps are consistent with the previous observa-
tions of OH-PLIF images, and the similar iso-velocity contours
above.
The analysis of the approximately 1000 instantaneous gas tem-
perature measurements obtained at each in situ measurement lo-
cation in the ﬂame zone

text:
deviation correlated with high turbulence regions 8,11. In this
study, the largest values of T occurred in the same location as the
largest values of mean velocity.
The effect of stoichiometry on ﬂame structure and stability can
be obtained by comparing Figs. 19a and 19b or Figs. 19c and
19d. In general, the higher heat release associated with the 
=0.80 cases resulted in 1 a reduction of the size of the highly
ﬂuctuating zone, and 2 a movement of that zone closer to the
injector inlet.
Probability distribution functions PDFs have been used to de-

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
scribe the characteristics of the ﬂuctuating temperature. Figures
20 and 21 show examples of gas temperature PDFs at axial loca-
tions of 40, 60, and of 80 mm and radial positions from the cen-
terline to a 30-mm radial location for the HS =0.80 and MS
=0.65 cases. Additional PDF results are included in the disser-
tation by Flores 2. Labeled on the PDF are the axial position
radial position mean gas temperature K standard deviation tem-
perature K and normalized standard deviation temperature, T
%.
In Fig. 20, the PDFs generally do not exhibit a Gaussian distri-
bution. The distributions in a single identiﬁable zone i.e., in ei-
ther the central recirculation zone or the side recirculation zone
seem to follow a Beta distribution. As with the velocity distribu-
tions, the distributions that bridged the two recirculation zones
tended to have a bimodal PDF. Comparison of distributions E and
F 40 mm in Fig. 20 show that these two PDF distributions are
clearly on opposite sides of the boundary between the central and
side recirculation zones. At a higher position 80 mm, distribu-
tions M, N, and O all exhibit a wide distribution that has a bimo-
dal character. This suggests that in the higher part of the ﬂame
there was a signiﬁcant side to side ﬂuctuation, even though the T
was not large, that shifted the side and central recirculation zones
back and forth across the diagnostic locations.
Figure 21 presents similar PDF results for the HS =0.80 case.
Fig. 18
Contour maps of mean gas temperatures for the four test conditions
Fig. 19
Contour maps of normalized /T% for the four test conditions

title: Department of Chemical Engineering and
text:
Since the vortex is more coalesced near the injector for the HS
=0.80 case, the bimodal character is easier to identify. Attention
is drawn to locations A and B z=40 mm where there is a bimo-
dal character to the PDF distribution even though these locations
are near the centerline. Locations C, D, and E also at 40 mm
reﬂect distributions in the high velocity region where cooler un-
reacted gases are being brought into the combustion chamber.
Location F shows a bimodal character, but seems to be dominated
by a higher temperature region associated with the side recircula-
tion zone. The distributions at locations G, H, and I z=60 mm
are dominated by a high-temperature zone associated with the
central recirculation zone. However, at locations J, K, and L also
at 60 mm, the distributions have the bimodal character associated
with components from both the central and side recirculation
zones. Higher in the burner z=80 mm the distributions are
nearly Gaussian in shape, and of nearly the same magnitude. This
suggests that for this test condition, the combustion was nearly
complete and near uniform ﬂow had been achieved. In Fig. 20 at
z=80 mm, the mean temperatures showed greater variation with
radius, and were much below the ﬁnal peak temperature in the
burner. The location of the PDFs with bimodal distributions is
shown in Fig. 22, showing that the bimodal distributions occur in
the strong shear layers between the central and side recirculation
zones.
Summary and Conclusion
A laboratory-scale gas turbine combustor LSGTC was used to
reproduce the characteristics found in a modern annular gas tur-
bine combustor. This laboratory scale gas turbine combustor was
used to investigate the characteristics of a premixed natural gas/air
ﬂame using PLIF, LDA, and CARS at four operating conditions
two swirl conditions, HS and MS, and two equivalence ratios,
=0.65 and 0.80. These data comprise one of the most complete
characterizations of ﬂames in lean premixed systems

ally across the two zones to provide a volumetric ﬂow rate in the
reverse direction in each recirculation zone. This analysis showed
that the ﬂame stabilization was strongly inﬂuenced by the central
recirculation zone for the HS =0.80 case, and by the side recir-
culation zone for the MS =0.65 case.

by a U.S. Department of Energy’s DOE Advanced Turbine Sys-
tems ATS program, with additional support by the Brigham
Young University BYU Advanced Combustion Engineering Re-
search Center ACERC.

r  radius

w  width
  equivalence ratio
Subscripts

s  side referring to side recirculation zone
u  axial velocity
v  radial velocity
w  tangential velocity
x  horizontal distance from centerline
z  vertical distance from burner surface
